reasoning:
Looking at this problem deeply, I need to think about what makes traditional retirement systems fail for variable income earners.

**The fundamental tension**: Traditional retirement systems demand consistent behavior from people living inconsistent lives. Every existing approach I can think of tries to make variable earners *act like* stable earners - contribute regularly, decide amounts, maintain discipline. This is fighting against reality rather than embracing it.

**Existing solutions to avoid**: Since none are listed, I'll note the implicit approaches I'm deliberately avoiding:
- Flexible contribution accounts (still require decisions each period)
- Micro-savings apps (still require attention and choices)
- Government mandates on gig platforms (still treats this as an enforcement problem)
- Matching incentives (still assumes workers can contribute consistently)

**The core insight I'm building from**: The problem isn't *how* to collect variable contributions - it's that we're asking for *decisions* from people whose cognitive bandwidth is consumed by income uncertainty. The solution must require **zero ongoing decisions**.

```json
{
  "label": "Zero-Decision Volatility-Indexed Retirement",
  "design_principles": "Consistency is the enemy. Remove all decisions. Make volatility itself the funding signal, not the obstacle. Capture windfalls automatically; protect shortfalls automatically.",
  "core_mechanism": "Universal automatic withholding at payment source with rates that adjust by algorithm based on payment size relative to rolling average—no human decisions at any point.",
  "how_it_works": "All payers (platforms, clients, employers) must withhold retirement % at payment. Rate formula: base_rate × income_position_factor. Payment >150% of your rolling average: 18% withheld. Payment near average: 10%. Payment <50% average: 3%. If 3-month rolling income drops below 60% of historical, system auto-disburses bridge payments from your fund—no application. When income recovers, withholding resumes. Worker never logs in, never decides, never applies. The math handles everything. Implementation requires: mandate on payment platforms, centralized contribution tracking, rolling-average calculation infrastructure.",
  "what_is_new": "Removes ALL decisions from the worker. Most systems reduce decisions; this eliminates them. Also: contribution rate is dynamic per-payment, not per-month or per-year.",
  "why_it_works": "Variable earners' scarcest resource isn't money—it's cognitive bandwidth. Every decision about 'should I contribute this month' competes with urgent survival decisions. By removing decisions, we capture windfalls people would otherwise spend and protect them in downturns without the shame of 'asking for help.'",
  "why_it_fails": "Paternalism concerns are real—this removes agency. Cross-platform withholding requires unprecedented coordination and creates surveillance infrastructure. Gaming risk: people might structure payments to minimize withholding. Political resistance from platforms who'd bear implementation costs.",
  "medium_term": "Payment platforms build withholding infrastructure. Variable workers begin accumulating retirement for first time. New norm emerges: all income has automatic retirement component, like taxes.",
  "long_term_vision": "Retirement participation becomes universal regardless of work arrangement. The gig economy's flexibility no longer comes at the cost of old-age security. 'I can't afford to save' disappears as a concept because saving isn't a choice—it's embedded in receiving payment itself. Variable work becomes genuinely viable as a lifetime career, not just a young person's gamble."
}
```

solution.label: Zero-Decision Volatility-Indexed Retirement
solution.design_principles: Consistency is the enemy. Remove all decisions. Make volatility itself the funding signal, not the obstacle. Capture windfalls automatically; protect shortfalls automatically.
solution.core_mechanism: Universal automatic withholding at payment source with rates that adjust by algorithm based on payment size relative to rolling average—no human decisions at any point.
solution.how_it_works: All payers (platforms, clients, employers) must withhold retirement % at payment. Rate formula: base_rate × income_position_factor. Payment >150% of your rolling average: 18% withheld. Payment near average: 10%. Payment <50% average: 3%. If 3-month rolling income drops below 60% of historical, system auto-disburses bridge payments from your fund—no application. When income recovers, withholding resumes. Worker never logs in, never decides, never applies. The math handles everything. Implementation requires: mandate on payment platforms, centralized contribution tracking, rolling-average calculation infrastructure.
solution.what_is_new: Removes ALL decisions from the worker. Most systems reduce decisions; this eliminates them. Also: contribution rate is dynamic per-payment, not per-month or per-year.
solution.why_it_works: Variable earners' scarcest resource isn't money—it's cognitive bandwidth. Every decision about 'should I contribute this month' competes with urgent survival decisions. By removing decisions, we capture windfalls people would otherwise spend and protect them in downturns without the shame of 'asking for help.'
solution.why_it_fails: Paternalism concerns are real—this removes agency. Cross-platform withholding requires unprecedented coordination and creates surveillance infrastructure. Gaming risk: people might structure payments to minimize withholding. Political resistance from platforms who'd bear implementation costs.
solution.medium_term: Payment platforms build withholding infrastructure. Variable workers begin accumulating retirement for first time. New norm emerges: all income has automatic retirement component, like taxes.
solution.long_term_vision: Retirement participation becomes universal regardless of work arrangement. The gig economy's flexibility no longer comes at the cost of old-age security. 'I can't afford to save' disappears as a concept because saving isn't a choice—it's embedded in receiving payment itself. Variable work becomes genuinely viable as a lifetime career, not just a young person's gamble.
